title: Lambda basics
instruction:
Lambda syntax:

```cpp
[capture](params) -> returnType { body }
```

- `[]` — capture nothing
- `[=]` — capture all by value
- `[&]` — capture all by reference
- Return type is optional (deduced)

Run this.
successMessage: Lambdas are anonymous functions. The compiler deduces the return type automatically. Lambdas are how we passed the sort comparator in Chapter 17 without naming a function.

title: Captures — access outer variables
successMessage: Threshold captured by value — a copy. Changing threshold later doesn't affect the lambda. sum captured by reference — the lambda modifies the outer sum directly.

title: std::function — store any callable
instruction:
`std::function<returnType(params)>` can hold ANY callable — lambda, function pointer, or functor:

```cpp
function<int(int)> doubler = [](int n){ return n * 2; };
doubler = someNamedFunction;  // can swap to a named function
```

This is how callbacks, event handlers, and strategy patterns work in C++.
successMessage: std::function stores any callable with the matching signature. applyToAll works with any transformation lambda. This is the callback/event handler pattern used in GUIs, async systems, and game engines.

title: Returning lambdas
successMessage: makeMultiplier captures factor in the returned lambda. counter1 and counter2 have independent counts. Closures capture state — this is how currying, event handlers, and callbacks maintain context.

title: Build a function pipeline
instruction:
No starter code.

Build a `Pipeline<T>` class that chains transformations:

```cpp
Pipeline<int>(numbers)
    .filter([](int n){ return n > 5; })
    .transform([](int n){ return n * 2; })
    .forEach([](int n){ cout << n << endl; });
```

Use `vector<function<...>>` to store the steps, or apply each in chain methods.
hint: Store vector<T> as data. filter returns a new Pipeline with filtered data. transform returns a new Pipeline with transformed data. forEach calls the function on each element.

codeChecks.message: Add filter() method taking a lambda predicate.
Add transform() method taking a lambda function.
Use lambdas or std::function for the pipeline steps.

successMessage: A fluent pipeline using lambdas. filter+transform+forEach is map/filter/reduce. This pattern is used in LINQ (C#), Java Streams, and Rust iterators — all inspired by functional programming. Chapter 20 done!
